findings.finding: The document body (research sections, decisions, and implementation notes) establishes that /update must cover pull/install, relink, binary-target verification, and restart handoff for both dev and installed modes, directly addressing the Then clause.

The research section explicitly compares /update against install-pi.sh, the child-node outcomes summarize the reconciliation, and the decision rationale frames the remaining difference (restart handoff vs hot-swap) as an intentional safe boundary choice rather than an accidental omission.

The decision and implementation notes explicitly require relink and binary-target verification in /update for dev mode, and the file scope targets extensions/bootstrap/index.ts and bin/pi.mjs for this purpose.

The research section and singular-package-runtime-boundary child node establish that installed mode resolves forked packages from npm, not from vendor/pi-mono. The decision reinforces vendor/pi-mono as dev-only.

The decision explicitly states update parity should end with a restart handoff rather than in-process reload, and the implementation constraint confirms package/runtime mutation must not rely on hot reload as the authoritative completion path.

All three children (pi-fork-worktree-reconciliation, singular-package-runtime-boundary, update-lifecycle-parity) are status resolved and their conclusions are summarized in the Child-node outcomes research section.
Implementation Notes file scope lists 5 files with descriptions and actions.
The research section distinguishes /update (full lifecycle) from /refresh (lightweight cache clear + reload alias), and the /update architecture fact in memory confirms this separation.
The decision and research consistently frame Omegon as the single installed product boundary with vendor/pi-mono as dev-only implementation source.
